Executive Summary: The project analyzes customer engagement, product utilization, relationship strength, and retention scoring to support churn reduction strategy.
The customer base contains 10,000 customers with an overall churn rate of 20.37% and retention rate of 79.63%.
Retention strength tiers clearly separate high-risk and low-risk customers, with Critical Retention Risk showing the highest churn exposure.
Inactive customers show weaker retention behavior and require earlier engagement-focused intervention.
Product utilization patterns show that more products do not always guarantee better retention, especially for high product-load customers.
High Product Load Risk is the highest churn-risk segment and should be treated as a priority intervention group.
Relationship strength is one of the clearest retention drivers, with churn decreasing as relationship strength improves.
Financial indicators such as salary-balance mismatch and credit card ownership provide supporting context but should not be used alone as retention predictors.
The business should combine retention score, engagement behavior, product depth, risk segment, and relationship strength to prioritize retention actions.
